feed.title: Deep Disquiet
feed.subtitle: Essays and notes on democracy, accountability, and the rule of law.

entry.title: Test Post
entry.content: This is a test post for Deep Disquiet. Use it to verify that the site builds and deploys correctly after pushing. If you see this line, the latest deploy (including DanubeData) is live.
entry.summary: This is a test post for Deep Disquiet. Use it to verify that the site builds and deploys correctly after pushing. If you see this line, the latest deploy (including DanubeData) is live.

entry.title: Corporations as Persons: Rights and Criminal Responsibility

link.title: Corporations as Persons: Rights and Criminal Responsibility

entry.content:
Working title. Draft with citations from dx/essay-research-findings-corporations-as-persons.md.

Introduction

Corporations are legal persons. They hold rights—to contract, to own property, to speak, and under Citizens United to spend on political campaigns. They are sued and regulated as persons. When they cause harm, we expect them to bear civil and sometimes administrative liability. But when it comes to criminal responsibility, the same legal persons are often treated with less seriousness than natural persons who cause comparable harm. This essay argues for symmetry: if a corporation is a person for rights and civil purposes, it should be a person for criminal purposes too—investigated, charged, and punished with seriousness that fits the harm. That implies enforcement parity, entity-level consequences (fines, probation, suspension, oversight, dissolution where appropriate), and accountability for the people in charge: officers and boards should face criminal liability when the company commits serious crimes on their watch. It also implies aligning rights with responsibilities—for example, restricting political spending by corporations that have been convicted of crimes, as a corporate analogue to felony disenfranchisement.

1. Civil and Constitutional Personhood

Corporations enjoy legal personhood for a wide range of purposes. They enter contracts, hold property, sue and are sued in tort. They have constitutional rights: First Amendment protection for speech, including political spending (Citizens United v. FEC); Fourth Amendment and due process; right to counsel and jury trial; protection against excessive fines. They have limited liability and a separate juridical identity from shareholders and officers—they are, in law, persons for the purposes of holding rights and bearing civil liability (CRS R43293; Citizens United v. FEC).

That personhood is not conditional on good behavior. It is the default. The question this essay presses is whether the same person should be held to the same standard when it breaks the law (CRS R43293; Citizens United v. FEC, 558 U.S. 310 (2010)).

2. Criminal Liability Today

Under U.S. federal law, corporations can be criminally liable. A corporation is liable for federal crimes committed by employees or agents (1) within the scope of employment and (2) with intent to benefit the company (CRS R43293). The U.S. Sentencing Guidelines (Chapter 8) apply to organizations convicted of felonies and Class A misdemeanors. Penalties include fines, probation, restitution, forfeiture, and restrictions on commercial activity. Corporations cannot be jailed; the entity has no body to incarcerate (USSC Ch. 8; DOJ Justice Manual 9-28.000).

In practice, enforcement is highly discretionary. The DOJ decides whether to prosecute; factors include seriousness, corporate history, compliance programs, and cooperation. Many matters are resolved through deferred prosecution agreements (DPAs) or guilty pleas rather than trial (CRS R43293; DOJ Principles). Individuals—officers and employees—remain separately liable for their own crimes, but corporate prosecution does not substitute for individual accountability; the two can and should run in parallel. The gap is that corporate criminal enforcement is often perceived and practiced as a softer track: fines and agreements rather than the full weight of the criminal law.

3. The Asymmetry

There is a persistent asymmetry. Civil: Corporations are routinely persons for rights (speech, property, due process) and for civil liability (sued, fined by regulators). Criminal: Corporations can be prosecuted and punished, but in practice enforcement is discretionary, resolutions are often settlements, there is no incarceration for the entity, and corporate crime is often treated in public and policy discourse as less grave than street crime—an echo of the white-collar parity problem (Coffee, “Crime and the Corporation: Making the Punishment Fit the Corporation”).

The logical claim is symmetry. If the corporation is a person for rights and civil duties, it should be a person for criminal duties too: investigated and punished with the same seriousness as natural persons for analogous harm, within the limits of what can be done to an entity. No jail, but fines, probation, suspension, receivership, or dissolution where the violation and history warrant. The “person” that enjoys First Amendment and property rights should bear full criminal accountability when it commits serious crimes.

4. Officers and Board: Liability on Their Watch

The entity cannot be jailed, but the people who run it can. A second dimension of symmetry is officer and board liability when the company commits serious crimes on their watch.

Current law already recognizes some of this. Officers are liable when they personally commit, direct, aid/abet, or conspire in the crime (CRS R43293). In addition, the Responsible Corporate Officer (RCO) doctrine—United States v. Dotterweich, 320 U.S. 277 (1943), United States v. Park, 421 U.S. 658 (1975)—allows conviction of executives in certain regulatory contexts (e.g. Federal Food, Drug and Cosmetic Act) without proof of personal intent or knowledge. The elements are: (1) position of responsibility and authority, (2) duty to prevent violations and implement measures so they do not occur, (3) “responsible relation to the situation.” So “on their watch” liability already exists in narrow, strict-liability settings.

The gap is that for most federal crimes—fraud, bribery, environmental crime, platform or audit violations—prosecutors must prove the officer’s own mens rea or direct participation. There is no general rule that the CEO, COO, or board are criminally liable simply because the company committed a serious crime and they had the authority to prevent or stop it.

The policy direction is to extend RCO-style or recklessness-based liability to more categories of corporate crime. When the company commits a serious crime and senior officers or the board had authority to prevent it and failed to take reasonable steps—or recklessly disregarded known risks—they should face criminal liability. The entity pays fines, probation, or suspension; the people in charge face the possibility of incarceration. That makes accountability personal as well as corporate and aligns with the idea that corporations as persons cannot be the only ones “punished”—the humans who run them must be accountable too.

5. One-Pager and Platform Accountability

This project’s verification and audit one-pager proposes a regime in which platforms have a verification duty, face independent audit, and incur penalties for fake accounts and coordinated or state-run campaigns—with escalation to “three strikes” and then suspension or placement under an independent entity (dx/verification-and-audit-penalty-one-pager.md).

That regime is a serious, person-like consequence for the corporate person: loss of liberty to operate, or temporary loss of control—analogous in severity to felony-level consequences for individuals. Framing the max penalty (suspension or oversight) as a serious violation, and three strikes as warranting it, aligns with the principle that corporations as persons should face criminal or quasi-criminal consequences when they repeatedly or egregiously break the rules. One can go further: define certain willful or repeated platform violations—e.g. knowingly tolerating state-run campaigns, or falsifying audit results—as criminal offenses for the corporation, with fines and/or court-ordered oversight (receiver, monitor) as the sanction. Then the corporate person is not only civilly or administratively penalized but criminally accountable.

6. Citizens United and Symmetry

Citizens United held that corporations have First Amendment rights to spend on political campaigns. If corporations have those rights, they should bear corresponding civic and criminal responsibility when they misuse the information environment—e.g. by hosting state-run campaigns, systemic inauthenticity, or other violations that undermine democratic discourse. Rights and responsibilities should be aligned.

One concrete proposal is corporate disenfranchisement: restrict or bar political spending by corporations that have been convicted of crimes, mirroring the collateral consequence of felony disenfranchisement for natural persons. The same “person” that has speech rights would then bear a consequence after criminal conviction. This has been proposed in the academic literature (e.g. Michigan Business & Entrepreneurial Law Review, “Crafting a Corporate Analogue to Criminal Disenfranchisement”); no jurisdiction has enacted it yet (dx/corporations-as-persons.md). It could be implemented by statute and would likely face a First Amendment challenge; the reply is that content-neutral restrictions tied to criminal conviction, applied to the same entity that enjoys the right, are a permissible alignment of personhood—rights and responsibilities together (Citizens United v. FEC).

7. Conclusion and Policy Implications

Corporations are persons for rights and for civil liability. They should be persons for criminal responsibility too: same seriousness in enforcement, consequences that fit the entity, and accountability for the humans in charge.

Policy implications:

  1. Enforcement parity — Treat corporate crime with the same investigative and prosecutorial seriousness as analogous harm by natural persons; reduce reliance on DPAs where a criminal conviction and sentencing would be more appropriate.

  2. Entity-level consequences — Use the full range of Ch. 8 and statutory options: fines, probation, restitution, forfeiture, suspension of activities, court-ordered oversight (monitor, receiver), and in the most egregious or repeated cases, dissolution or equivalent. Three-strikes-style escalation (as in the verification one-pager) fits this.

  3. Officer and board liability — Extend RCO-style or recklessness-based criminal liability to more categories of corporate crime so that CEOs, COOs, and boards face prosecution when the company commits serious crimes and they had authority to prevent or stop it.

  4. Corporate disenfranchisement — Enact statutes that restrict or bar political spending by corporations convicted of crimes, as a corporate analogue to felony disenfranchisement, so that the same person that has speech rights bears collateral consequences after conviction.

Objections can be anticipated. “Punishing the corporation hurts workers and shareholders”—fines and probation can be designed with restitution and compliance; officer liability targets decision-makers; suspension is for repeat or egregious cases. “Officer liability will chill risk-taking”—the standard can be recklessness or known failure to prevent, not strict liability for all crimes. “Corporate disenfranchisement violates the First Amendment”—the reply is symmetry: same person, same responsibilities, content-neutral restriction tied to conviction.

Corporations as persons is not only a doctrinal category; it is a commitment to holding the same legal persons to one standard of accountability. The next step is to make that standard real in enforcement, sentencing, and the liability of the people who run them.

References

Full citations and URLs are in dx/essay-research-findings-corporations-as-persons.md. Key sources: Citizens United v. FEC, 558 U.S. 310 (2010); DOJ Justice Manual 9-28.000 (Principles of Federal Prosecution of Business Organizations); USSC Guidelines Ch. 8; CRS R43293 (Corporate Criminal Liability: An Overview of Federal Law); United States v. Dotterweich, 320 U.S. 277 (1943); United States v. Park, 421 U.S. 658 (1975); John C. Coffee Jr., “Crime and the Corporation: Making the Punishment Fit the Corporation”; Michigan B. & Entrep. L. Rev. (corporate disenfranchisement proposal); dx/verification-and-audit-penalty-one-pager.md.
entry.summary: Working title. Draft with citations from dx/essay-research-findings-corporations-as-persons.md.

entry.title: White Collar Crime Parity and Official Enhancements

link.title: White Collar Crime Parity and Official Enhancements

entry.content:
Working title. Draft with citations from dx/essay-research-findings-white-collar-parity.md.

Introduction

A democracy depends on the idea that the law applies equally. When some crimes are investigated and punished with real seriousness and others are treated as second-tier, equal treatment under law is compromised—and so is democratic legitimacy. White collar crime is today systematically under-investigated and under-punished compared with violent street crime. That disparity is a democratic deficit. This essay argues two things: first, that white collar crimes should be investigated and punished with the same seriousness as violent crimes; second, that government officials who commit white collar crimes should be subject to aggravating enhancements—similar to weapon-use enhancements—because abuse of public office multiplies harm and undermines trust in government itself.

The argument does not depend on equating a fraud with a murder in every respect. It depends on the claim that the system of enforcement and punishment should not treat white collar offending as inherently less serious, and that when the offender is a public official, the abuse of office is an aggravating factor that the law should recognize in a clear, principle-driven way, much as it already does for the use of a weapon.

1. Disparities: Sentencing, Perception, and Resources

White collar offenders receive systematically lighter sentences than those convicted of violent street crime. Studies document that judges often impose terms in the range of roughly six months to three years for serious white collar offenses, while comparable culpability and harm in violent cases lead to substantially longer terms. Rationales offered for this gap include the “psychological punitiveness” of prison for white collar defendants and the goal of “deterrence of peers”—but the result is a persistent differential by crime type and offender class (OJP, “White-Collar vs Street Crime Sentencing Disparity: How Judges See the Problem”; NIJ, “Sentencing the White-Collar Offender”).

Public perception is nuanced. When violent and white collar crime are contrasted directly, respondents tend to rate violent crime as more serious. When white collar harm is presented on its own—with scope of financial loss, number of victims, or institutional damage made clear—perceived seriousness can match or exceed that of violent crime (Piquero et al.; Michel, Critical Criminology 2015). So the gap in punishment is not a simple reflection of public judgment; it reflects institutional and legal choices.

The foundational critique goes back to Edwin Sutherland. In his 1939 address and later in White Collar Crime (1949), he argued that laws and sanctions are differentially applied, to the benefit of the white collar offender. That is not only a fairness problem; it is a democratic equality problem. Equal treatment under law is undermined when outcomes depend systematically on crime type and the social position of the offender.

A further dimension is resources. Congress has cut funding for investigating certain white collar crimes, including tax evasion, and the FBI and DOJ have at times deprioritized white-collar and public-corruption work in favor of other priorities—contributing to a resource imbalance between white collar and violent-crime enforcement (e.g. Reuters/DOJ reporting, 2025, on FBI immigration prioritization and Criminal Division narrowing of white-collar enforcement). The combination of lighter sentences, differential perception framing, and fewer investigative resources creates a triple disparity: white collar crime is treated as less serious in sentencing, in public discourse, and in the allocation of enforcement capacity. That triple disparity is what this essay labels a democratic deficit.

2. Harm Parity: Why White Collar Crime Warrants Serious Response

If the case for parity rested only on abstract equality, it would be weaker. It is strengthened by the scale and nature of harm that white collar crime causes. Fraud alone has been estimated at around 5% of revenues (ACFE), implying over $1.3 trillion in annual fraud losses in the U.S. when applied to GDP; broader OJP estimates for white collar and corporate crime run from $426 billion to $1.7 trillion annually. Single cases—Enron-style frauds, massive Ponzi schemes—have caused tens of billions in losses and devastated thousands of victims (ACFE Report to the Nations; OJP, “How Much Does White Collar Crime Cost?”).

The harms are not only financial. Victims of fraud and corporate crime report emotional and psychological impact comparable to that associated with street crime: loss of security, trauma, and lasting damage to trust. Beyond individual victims, white collar crime erodes institutional trust, destabilizes markets, destroys jobs, and in the case of environmental or safety-related corporate crime, causes serious public health harm. Scholarly work has argued that when intangibles and systemic effects are included, total victimization costs from white collar crime can exceed those of street crime (e.g. Springer, “Examining the multifaceted harms of corporate and white-collar crime”).

None of this requires claiming that every white collar offense is “worse” than every violent offense. It requires the more modest claim that the seriousness of white collar crime—in aggregate and in many individual cases—warrants investigation and punishment comparable in seriousness to violent crime. A system that consistently treats white collar crime as second-tier, despite the scale of harm, is a system that has chosen to under-enforce and under-punish by category. Parity in response is therefore justified on grounds of both equality and harm.

3. Weapon Enhancements as a Model

Federal and state law already use aggravating factors to increase sentences when commission of a crime involves greater culpability or greater danger. A standard example is the use of a weapon. Under 18 U.S.C. § 924(c), federal law imposes mandatory minimums for possession of a firearm in connection with certain crimes (e.g., five years), brandishing (seven years), and discharge (ten years). The purpose is deterrence and a heightened penalty for the use of force or instruments of harm. Similarly, sentencing guidelines (§ 2K2.4) and state codes (e.g., California Penal Code § 12022) add enhancements when a crime is committed with a weapon—the base offense is augmented because the manner of commission increased danger and abused an instrument of harm (18 U.S.C. § 924(c); USSC Quick Facts, Section 924(c)).

The structure is simple: committing a crime with X can add a defined enhancement. The analogy proposed here is that committing a white collar crime as a government official or in official capacity should also add an enhancement. The reasons are parallel: abuse of public trust, greater institutional harm, and damage to democratic legitimacy. Just as the law treats “commission with a weapon” as an aggravating circumstance, it should treat “commission by a public official” as an aggravating circumstance for white collar crime—predictable, principle-driven, and applied consistently rather than ad hoc.

4. Public Officials Today: Existing Law and the Gap

Current law already recognizes some forms of official wrongdoing in sentencing. The U.S. Sentencing Guidelines include § 3B1.3, which applies a two-level enhancement when the defendant abused a position of public or private trust in a way that significantly facilitated the offense. It applies where the defendant had “professional or managerial discretion.” There are also substantial increases for high-level or sensitive official bribery (e.g., eight-level increase for bribes to judges, prosecutors, or agency heads), and § 3A1.2 provides a three-level increase when the victim is a government officer (or family) targeted because of official status (USSC Guidelines Manual Ch. 3, § 3B1.3; United States v. Douglas).

High-profile cases show both that serious sentences are possible and that outcomes are uneven. Senator Menendez received 11 years; Congressman Santos, 87 months; former New York official Joseph Percoco, 6 years. Other officials have received far lighter treatment—home detention and fines—in cases that involved clear abuse of office. The point is not that every official gets a pass, but that the system is reactive and patchy rather than systematic. Enhancement turns on particular offense types (e.g., bribery) or on proving “abuse of trust” in the commission of the offense, rather than on the simple fact that the offender was a government official who committed a white collar crime in the course of or by virtue of office.

The gap, then, is this. Current law focuses on “abuse of trust” and “sensitive roles” in specific offenses. This essay argues for a general “official capacity” enhancement: any white collar crime committed by a government official should receive an enhancement, analogous to “committed with a weapon.” The grounds are (a) that harm to democratic trust is inherent when an official commits such a crime, and (b) that parity with how we treat weapon use—clear, statutory, and predictable—would make official accountability more consistent and more legitimate.

5. Democracy and Legitimacy

Why should democracies care about this beyond fairness? Because the failure to punish official white collar crime is not only unfair; it is destabilizing. Research has shown that high-level political corruption causally erodes support for democracy, increases preference for authoritarian alternatives, and can sharply increase violent protest (Jha, Rivera & Seira, “Democracy Corrupted: Apex Corruption and the Erosion of Democratic Values,” Stanford 2024—event study: support for democracy fell, support for authoritarianism rose 11%, violent protests rose 70% after apex scandals; field experiment: exposure to corruption reduced democratic support, trust, turnout, and volunteering). So under-enforcement and under-punishment of official misconduct are not merely distributive injustices; they undermine the legitimacy of the democratic order itself.

Trust in government is already low in many democracies. OECD and US surveys show that trust correlates with perceived corruption; in OECD countries only about 39% report high or moderately high trust in national government, and trust has declined (OECD Survey on Drivers of Trust, 2024). Where corruption is seen as unpunished, voting, volunteering, and trust in fellow citizens tend to fall. Consistent investigation and punishment of official misconduct function as legitimacy-preserving: they signal that the law applies to everyone, including elites.

Finally, equal citizenship is a core democratic idea. The law must be seen to apply equally. Differential leniency for white collar and official offenders undermines that principle; parity in investigation and punishment, and explicit “official capacity” enhancements, reinforce it. The argument for parity and for official enhancements is therefore not only criminal-policy argument but democratic necessity.

6. Conclusion and Policy Implications

This essay has argued that in a democracy, white collar crimes should be investigated and punished with the same seriousness as violent crimes, and that government officials who commit white collar crimes should be subject to aggravating enhancements—like weapon-use enhancements—because abuse of public office multiplies harm and undermines democratic legitimacy.

Policy implications:

  1. Investigation parity. Allocate and prioritize resources so that white collar and official misconduct are investigated with seriousness comparable to violent crime. That may require rebalancing FBI/DOJ and prosecutorial focus, not only adding resources but treating white collar enforcement as a core legitimacy function.

  2. Sentencing parity. Reduce the systematic gap between white collar and violent-crime sentencing so that seriousness of harm and culpability drive outcomes, rather than crime type or offender class. This includes revisiting guideline and statutory ranges where they embed current disparities.

  3. Statutory “official capacity” enhancement. Enact a general enhancement for white collar crimes committed by government officials in the course of or by virtue of office—similar in structure to weapon enhancements. Define “government official” and “in the course of office” clearly (existing § 3B1.3 and bribery enhancements can serve as templates) so that the enhancement is predictable and not overused.

Objections can be anticipated. “White collar offenders are different”—but deterrence and retribution attach to harm and culpability, which can be as great as in violent crime; “different” treatment risks class bias. “Enhancements will be overused”—hence the need for clear definitions and scope tied to existing enhancement design. “Resources are limited”—democratic legitimacy justifies rebalancing toward white collar and official misconduct rather than accepting the current disparity as inevitable.

Equal treatment under law and the legitimacy of democratic institutions both demand that we take white collar crime—and above all white collar crime by those who hold public office—as seriously as we take violent crime. The first essay in that project is to make the case for parity and for official enhancements. The next is to implement them.

References

Full citations and URLs are in dx/essay-research-findings-white-collar-parity.md. Key sources: OJP/NIJ (sentencing disparity, cost); ACFE (Report to the Nations, fraud cost); Springer (Michel 2015, perceived seriousness; multifaceted harms); Sutherland (1939 address, White Collar Crime 1949); USSC Guidelines (§ 3B1.3, § 2K2.4); 18 U.S.C. § 924(c); Jha, Rivera & Seira, Stanford (apex corruption and democracy); OECD (trust 2024); DOJ/FBI priorities (2025 reporting).
entry.summary: Working title. Draft with citations from dx/essay-research-findings-white-collar-parity.md.

entry.title: One-Pager: Platform Verification Duty + Audit-Based Penalty for Fake Accounts

link.title: One-Pager: Platform Verification Duty + Audit-Based Penalty for Fake Accounts

entry.content:
Published. Final.

Problem: Anonymous and pseudonymous accounts enable foreign actors to impersonate Americans and bots to deliver propaganda at scale. Voluntary verification and detection are insufficient.

Proposal: (1) Require covered platforms to operate verification systems. (2) Mandate independent audits; require prominent publication of audit results. (3) Impose penalties that scale with fake-account count/rate, plus a campaign penalty when coordinated/state-run campaigns are found. (4) Three strikes (max penalty 3×) → suspension or placement under an independent entity until remedied.

Verification duty

  • Platforms above a size/risk threshold must run verification (e.g., proof-of-personhood or “verified human”) so users can be “one person, one account” without disclosing identity.
  • Standard: reasonable care—adequate design and operation, not perfection. Preserves anonymity while reducing bots and impersonation.

Independent audit

  • Periodic (e.g., annual) audit by regulator or approved auditor: assess verification adequacy and estimate inauthentic-account count/rate using a statutory definition and standard methodology.
  • “Inauthentic” = bot, impersonation, or coordinated inauthentic behavior (definition in law; exclusions for parody/satire).
  • Publish results prominently: Platforms must publish each audit report in a prominent way (e.g., linked from homepage, in-app notice, dedicated transparency page)—not buried. Enables public and investor scrutiny.

Penalty options

  • Count-based: $X per inauthentic account above a de minimis threshold.
  • Rate-based: Escalating fines by % of MAU that is inauthentic (e.g., 2–5%, 5–10%, etc.).
  • Hybrid: Base fine for inadequate verification + surcharge scaling with inauthentic count/rate. Cap at Z% of global revenue (e.g., DSA-style).

Campaign penalty (on top of base)

  • If the audit finds a coordinated or state-run influence campaign that the platform should have detected and mitigated: add a lump-sum or tiered fine (e.g., coordinated campaign → $Y; state-run campaign → $Z), or a multiplier on the base fake-account penalty.
  • Audit methodology must explicitly assess campaign presence (e.g., graph/behavioral analysis, threat-intel). Platforms pay for both overall inauthentic prevalence and for failing to prevent or remove serious campaigns.

Escalation: three strikes (max penalty = serious violation)

  • Hitting the maximum penalty (cap) is treated as a serious violation. If a platform reaches the maximum penalty three times (e.g., three audit cycles): suspend the platform’s covered service (or relevant operations) or place it under an independent entity (receiver, trustee, or court-appointed monitor) until remediation is verified.
  • “Remedied” = the entity certifies that verification, detection, and governance are fixed; a follow-up audit shows compliance; and the regulator restores normal operation. Design details (who appoints the entity, scope of suspension, appeal) to be set in law.

Safeguards

  • Penalize only accounts a reasonable verification/detection system could have caught (or tie penalty to failure to verify + excess prevalence).
  • Transparent audit methodology; appeal and judicial review.
  • Avoid over-deletion: don’t reward platforms for purging legitimate anonymous users.

Precedent

  • FTC: per-violation fines for fake reviews/influence ($51,744/violation). DSA: independent audits + up to 6% global turnover. X’s €120M DSA fine included misleading verification (blue checks).

Ask: Enact a verification duty, require independent audits with prominent publication of results, impose base + campaign penalties, and escalate to suspension or independent oversight (receiver/trustee/monitor) when a platform hits the maximum penalty three times, until remediation is verified.

entry.title: Welcome
entry.content: This is the Deep Disquiet blog. Essays and notes on democracy, accountability, and the rule of law will appear here as they are published.
entry.summary: This is the Deep Disquiet blog. Essays and notes on democracy, accountability, and the rule of law will appear here as they are published.
